J1:
Why: The board website needed a free, reliable home. A GitHub repository is that home — it hosts the site's code and costs nothing.

How: This one's already done — you created github.com/racinel200/nook-listing-engine on 7/22. Nothing more to do.
J3:
Why: Claude needed permission to update the website file in your GitHub account on your behalf — a token is like a limited-access key that only opens this one repo.

How: Done. One housekeeping note: if you ever pasted a token into a chat, you can revoke it at github.com → Settings → Developer settings → Fine-grained tokens — the working key is stored securely outside of chat.
A1:
Why: The board only helps if you can see it — publishing the site made your to-do list something you can open on your phone instead of asking Claude what's next.

How: Done — the AI pushed the site files to GitHub and verified everything rendered.
J2:
Why: GitHub Pages is the free switch that turns the repository into a live public website.

How: Done — you flipped it on 7/22 and the board went live at racinel200.github.io/nook-listing-engine.
J4:
Why: This board is your single to-do list — every action item from every Claude conversation lands here. If it's not one tap away, it stops getting used, and the whole system quietly dies.

How: Open racinel200.github.io/nook-listing-engine on your phone. iPhone: Share button → Add to Home Screen. On your computer: bookmark it. Then tap one task circle to confirm marking things done works.
A2:
Why: Sellers hire the agent with the most impressive marketing plan, and none of your top three Dripping Springs competitors has a real listing-marketing page. This page is proof you launch listings harder than anyone — and it doubles as the spine of your listing presentation.

How: Done — the AI drafted the full page (photography, drone, twilight, video, staging, single-property site, Zillow Showcase, Meta ads, luxury syndication). It's waiting on your review in J5.
J5:
Why: This page is how you out-market every listing agent in Dripping Springs — it shows sellers exactly what they get when you launch their home, which none of your competitors puts in writing. It only earns money once it's live.

How: Ask Claude to show you the draft (it's saved in the project). Read it once, mark anything that doesn't sound like you, then send it to your web person for nook-realty.com — or have Claude prep the final copy. Reuse the same content in every listing presentation.
J6:
Why: Your career tops out at a $5.4M sale and ~$65M in volume, but a luxury seller Googling you can't see any of it. A sold gallery is the fastest way to make the track record visible — and it can't be built without the addresses you're comfortable showing.

How: Pick 4–6 past luxury sales (address + sold price you're OK publishing — MLS-public info only). Add photos if you have them. Reply in any Claude chat or leave them in the board's note box; that unlocks the AI to draft the gallery page.
A3:
Why: Luxury sellers vet you online before they ever call. One strong sold-gallery page plus a consistent credential blurb (CLHMS, ~$65M career, $5.4M top sale) everywhere — website, Instagram bio, email signature, listing deck — makes the vetting land in your favor.

How: Waits on J6. Once you send the solds, the AI drafts the gallery page and writes the credential stack in a version sized for each channel. You just review.
J7:
Why: You earned the CLHMS luxury designation, but you're not currently findable in the Institute's directory — where luxury sellers and referring agents actually look. If the membership lapsed, the badge is doing nothing.

How: Log in at luxuryhomemarketing.com → check membership status. If lapsed, renew; if current, make sure your directory profile is public and complete. Takes ~10 minutes. Tell Claude the outcome so the credential copy in A3 says it right.
A4:
Why: You have 8 Zillow reviews; the local leader has 77. Reviews are the #1 trust signal sellers check and part of how Zillow scores you. Engineered asks — nudging clients to mention the neighborhood, property type, and how fast it sold — make each review do search-ranking work too.

How: Done — the AI pulled 10 recent closed clients (Nook's only) and drafted a personal ask for each. Names stay in the private project, never on this board. Your part is J8.
J8:
Why: Going from 8 reviews toward 25–40 by Oct 31 is the single fastest credibility move available — it lifts Zillow ranking, Google presence, and seller confidence all at once. Ten drafts are sitting ready; only you can green-light sends.

How: Ask Claude to walk you through review batch #1. Approve or tweak each note (they're written in your voice), then have Claude place them in Gmail as drafts for you to send. 20 minutes, maybe less.
J9:
Why: When someone searches 'Dripping Springs listing agent', the Google Business Profile box is what they see first. Nobody can optimize yours until it's confirmed you actually own it.

How: Go to business.google.com signed in as jennifer@nookrealtygroup.com. Look for a Nook Realty / Jen Ferrara profile. If it exists, confirm you're the owner; if not, claim or create it (Google verifies by postcard, phone, or video). Tell Claude what you find — that unlocks A5.
A5:
Why: An optimized Google Business Profile — right categories, seller-focused description, services, regular posts, review link — is free local-search real estate your competitors are only half-using.

How: Waits on J9. Once you confirm access, the AI drafts the complete optimization package; you paste it in or approve Claude doing it with you.
J10:
Why: You have three Zillow profiles splitting your reviews and sales history — a seller checking you out sees a fraction of the real record. Zillow Pro is free for Preferred partners through 2026, so there's no reason not to grab it.

How: Log into Zillow → claim/activate Zillow Pro. Then look at your profiles and pick the keeper (likely the 5.0★ one). Tell Claude which — that unlocks the consolidation request in A6.
A6:
Why: One consolidated profile with all reviews and sales in one place is worth more than three partial ones — for sellers AND for Zillow's ranking algorithm.

How: Waits on J10. The AI drafts the consolidation request to Zillow support plus rewritten profile copy with Nook branding and Dripping Springs luxury positioning. You approve, Claude sends.
J11:
Why: The instant home-valuation funnel (A7) is the plan's main seller-lead engine, and RealScout's Home Value Alerts would power it at no new cost — IF the subscription is active. Otherwise the fallback costs $179–299/mo, which changes the math.

How: Try logging into RealScout. If it works, check billing/settings for who pays (you, Sprout, or a trial). If you can't get in, ask Victoria or your Sprout contact. Report back: active or not, and who pays.
A7:
Why: None of your competitors offers instant home valuation — it's the open lane. Homeowners who want a number are early-stage sellers; the timeframe question (now / 3 months / 12 months / just curious) sorts hot from warm so follow-up matches intent.

How: Waits on J11. The AI specs the landing page copy, the qualifying flow, and how each answer routes into Follow Up Boss stages and Flodesk nurture speeds.
A8:
Why: drippingspringsfamilies.com already has the audience; it just never asked homeowners anything. A 'What's My DSISD Home Worth?' page plus a gated 2026 Seller's Guide turns existing traffic into seller leads — using the real story (63.5% of DS listings cut price in Q2) that makes owners want expert help.

How: Done — both pages are drafted and waiting on your review in J12.
J12:
Why: This turns your best asset — DSF's local audience — into a seller-lead source, and it's also step one of leaving Azulio (the new form replaces theirs, so the funnel never goes dark).

How: Ask Claude to show the A8 drafts. Approve or edit, publish both pages on drippingspringsfamilies.com, and swap the Azulio form for the new one per the exit plan. Your web person can do the publishing if you'd rather.
J13:
Why: Meta ads are supposed to be a core lead channel, but zero Meta-source leads are reaching Nook queues — either nothing is running or the leads are leaking elsewhere. Nobody can build the seller campaign (A9) without knowing what access exists.

How: Two answers needed: (1) Are any Meta/Facebook/Instagram ad campaigns currently live? (2) Can you log into Ads Manager / Business Manager, and is the ad account yours or Sprout's? Check business.facebook.com or ask whoever ran past ads. Report both answers.
A9:
Why: Seller-side Meta ads (home-value and market-report angles, aimed at homeowners in DSISD/Wimberley/Driftwood zips) fill the listing pipeline directly instead of chasing buyer leads.

How: Waits on J13. The AI drafts 3 ad variants including the price-cut hook, the homeowner audience spec, a budget split, and FUB source tagging so results are measurable. Nothing spends without your approval.
A10:
Why: Geographic farming works when you pick ONE neighborhood and show up relentlessly. Picking on data — turnover rate, price band, competition — beats picking on gut feel, because a farm is an 18–24 month commitment.

How: Done — the AI scored three 300–400-home luxury pockets (La Ventana recommended over Saddletree Ranch and Spanish Oaks, Lost Creek assessed separately). The scorecard with verification steps is ready for your call in J14.
J14:
Why: The farm only compounds if it's ONE neighborhood, hit quarterly for 18–24 months (~$85–135/mo). Committing is the decision; the scorecard did the analysis.

How: Ask Claude for the A10 scorecard. La Ventana is the data pick — check the short verification list (drive it, confirm no dominant incumbent agent), or just confirm the pick. Telling Claude 'go with La Ventana' unlocks postcard #1 (A11).
A11:
Why: Postcard #1 sets the farm's tone: just-solds + one hyperlocal stat says 'this agent knows OUR streets', and the QR code to your valuation page makes response measurable — most agents mail vanity cards that can't be tracked.

How: Waits on J14. The AI writes the copy and a Canva design brief; you approve, then it goes to print (Canva or your printer of choice).
J15:
Why: The Dripping Springs playbook (community site → local authority → listings) is planned for Wimberley next, and wimberleyfamilies.com looked unregistered on 7/22. $12 now reserves the whole strategy's next chapter before anyone else grabs it.

How: Any registrar works (Namecheap, GoDaddy, Google Domains). Search wimberleyfamilies.com, buy 1 year (~$12), skip every add-on they offer. That's it — registering unlocks A12.
A12:
Why: A ready page map plus the first three community profiles means the Wimberley site launches from a running start, reusing everything the DSF playbook proved.

How: Waits on J15. Once the domain is yours, the AI scaffolds the site plan and drafts the first three profiles for your review.
A13:
Why: Consistent local-market video is the highest-leverage content in real estate: it builds listing authority, feeds Instagram/YouTube, and for luxury sellers the videos ARE your listing presentation. The format ('What actually sold in DSISD this month') writes itself from MLS data every month.

How: Done — 8-week calendar, complete script #1, and an editor brief (~$100–200/video, phone-shot, pro-edited). Your part is filming — J16.
J16:
Why: Video #1 starts the engine. Done beats perfect here — a real agent on a phone talking real numbers outperforms a polished ad, and every video ends on your valuation-page call to action.

How: Block ~90 minutes. Open the A13 script (ask Claude for it), shoot on your phone — natural light, quiet room or on location, phone horizontal at eye level. Don't aim for perfect takes; the editor fixes pacing. Send the raw file back and the editor brief handles the rest.
A14:
Why: DS ELITE Real Estate Professionals is visibly how NestHaven's top producers source referral business in Dripping Springs. If the dues are reasonable, it's a zero-ad-spend listing channel hiding in plain sight.

How: Nothing for you yet — the AI researches criteria, dues, who runs it, and how to apply, then hands you a join/skip recommendation in J17.
J17:
Why: Referral groups put you in the room where 'who should list my house?' gets answered. This one already works for your biggest competitor.

How: Waits on A14's research. Read the one-page summary, decide if dues make sense, apply if yes. Bonus: ask members what the Wimberley/Driftwood equivalent is.
A15:
Why: The Sentinel Peak effect: Veritas turned ONE developer relationship into 26 luxury homesites to sell. Small Hill Country builders and acreage developers are the highest-volume listing source a single coffee meeting can unlock.

How: Nothing for you yet — the AI builds a 5-name target list (DS/Driftwood/Wimberley builders, acreage developers, land sellers) with a contact and a specific conversation angle for each. Then you book two coffees (J18).
J18:
Why: One good builder relationship can out-produce a year of ad spend — these two coffees are the highest-leverage hours in the whole plan.

How: Waits on A15's list. Pick the two easiest warm-ups from the five names, use the drafted angle for each, and get them booked — coffee near their office beats asking them to come to you.
A16:
Why: Luxury listings follow life events — estate settlements, divorces, wealth moves — and the professionals handling those events (wealth managers, estate & divorce attorneys, builders, architects, stagers) decide who gets called. A deliberate referral circle beats waiting to be remembered.

How: Nothing for you yet — the AI builds a 10-contact list in Westlake/Lakeway/DS with a short personal outreach note drafted for each. You book the first two meetings (J19).
J19:
Why: One referral coffee or lunch a month is the pace that compounds; these are just the first two. Each relationship is a standing pipeline, not a transaction.

How: Waits on A16's list. Pick the two contacts you have the warmest path to, send the drafted note (or have Claude put it in Gmail for you), and get them on the calendar.
A17:
Why: Expired and withdrawn $750K+ listings are sellers who already raised their hand and got let down — and the current $2M+ market stall makes that list unusually rich. Almost nobody follows up with them properly; a hand-written note stands alone.

How: Nothing for you to do yet — the AI builds the tracker, a daily check workflow for your territories, the note draft, and the compliance checklist (confirm truly off-market, no exclusive agreement, DNC check) before anything reaches you.
A18:
Why: Zillow Showcase makes your listings visibly premium on the site sellers check most (~$30–80/listing), and Zillow Pro is free for Preferred partners — but both need your rep to quote and confirm. A ready-to-send email removes the friction.

How: Nothing for you yet — the AI drafts the email to your Zillow rep; it lands in J20 for your one-click approval.
J20:
Why: One email gets Showcase pricing and confirms your free Pro enrollment — both strengthen how listings (and you) look on Zillow.

How: Waits on A18. Claude puts the draft in your Gmail; you read it, tweak if you want, hit send.
J21:
Why: Two numbers in your Zillow Preferred agreement shape big decisions: the success-fee % (what each Zillow closing really costs) and whether the relationship is portable if you ever change brokerage. The broker-license question stays stuck until these are known.

How: Find the agreement — search your email for 'Zillow' + 'agreement'/'Flex'/'Preferred', check your files, or ask your Zillow rep for a copy. Send Claude the fee % and the portability language (or the doc itself, into the private project — never the public board).
A19:
Why: The shared Sprout Follow Up Boss account limits what you can change yourself. This ask finds out what you CAN control (lead routing, action plans, integrations) and requests the one rule that matters most: $750K+ Nook-bound leads route straight to you.

How: Nothing for you yet — the AI drafts the note to Sprout's admin. You approve and send in J22.
J22:
Why: Speed-to-lead is scored by Zillow and decides conversions — this answer determines whether automation gets set by you or requested from Sprout every time.

How: Waits on A19. Read the drafted ask, adjust tone if needed, send it to your Sprout admin contact (Claude can place it in Gmail).
A20:
Why: Zillow Preferred scores you on stage updates and responsiveness — stale 'Lead' stages literally cost ranking, which costs leads, which costs money. A regular sweep catches what slips.

How: Nothing for you to do — the AI reads Nook's contacts only (never other Sprout agents'), reports who's sat too long with proposed fixes. The report stays in the private project; no client names ever appear on this board. You approve fixes in J23.
J23:
Why: Two approvals in one: the proposed stage moves (Zillow score protection) and reconnecting the Facebook Lead Ads → Follow Up Boss feed so Meta leads stop vanishing.

How: Waits on A20's report. Review the proposed moves — a yes/no per item is enough — and give Claude the go-ahead. The feed fix may need one login from you.
A21:
Why: NestHaven ranks with a 'Best Realtors in Dripping Springs' article that contains zero data. A version with verifiable numbers — review counts, sales volume, days-on-market — is more honest, more useful, and built to outrank theirs while putting you in the conversation credibly.

How: Nothing for you yet — the AI writes it with sources for every claim. It lands in J24 for your careful read before anything publishes.
J24:
Why: An article ranking local agents — including you — is the most sensitive content in the plan. Published right, it's a permanent search asset; published carelessly, it's a neighbor-relations problem.

How: Waits on A21. Read every word (not a skim), check you're comfortable with how each named agent is represented, then publish on drippingspringsfamilies.com or send back edits.
A22:
Why: All three competitors lean on ranch/land authority — it's their moat. A genuinely useful acreage-seller guide (wells, septic, ag exemptions, wildlife valuations) attacks that moat with substance and ends on your valuation CTA.

How: Nothing for you to do — the AI writes it. It queues for your review before publishing like all content.
J25:
Why: JamesEdition-style luxury syndication is sold à la carte for real money — but your brokerage's ERA affiliation may already include some of it. Ten minutes of asking prevents paying twice.

How: Email or call your Sprout/ERA contact: 'What luxury listing syndication is included through the brokerage or the ERA network?' Send Claude the answer; it slots into the luxury launch package.
J26:
Why: Claude sessions read the project's docs to know how the board works. After the v2 upgrade (board moved into Google Sheets), stale docs would send future sessions down retired paths.

How: Done 7/22 — v2 files saved over the old ones and retired docs removed. (The docs were refreshed again when the Explain-this feature shipped.)
A23:
Why: The July audit found real credibility leaks on nookrealtygroup.com — a wrong Zillow link, a New-York-area phone number, an outdated team page, dead links, undated posts, no lead magnets. Each one quietly costs trust or leads; a single punch list gets them all fixed in one pass.

How: Nothing for you yet — the AI compiles every flagged item into a ready-to-send list for your web person, ordered by impact. You forward it in J27.
J27:
Why: The fix list only fixes things once it reaches whoever edits the site — one forward turns the audit into results.

How: Waits on A23. Skim the punch list, then email it to your web person (or apply the fixes yourself in Luxury Presence — most are small). Claude can draft the email.
J28:
Why: nookaustin.com is a stale Side-era site still live, and ferrarahomes.com points at it — old branding competing with your real site in search results and confusing anyone who lands there. One canonical brand everywhere.

How: Log into the registrar where those domains live → set both to redirect (301/permanent forward) to nookrealtygroup.com. Every registrar has a 'forwarding' setting; Claude can give exact steps if you say which registrar.
A24:
Why: Azulio (GoHighLevel) is a monthly cost the plan replaces, but leaving carelessly could break forms or lose contacts. A proper exit plan — export everything, confirm what's actually hosted there, then send notice — makes it clean.

How: Nothing for you yet — the AI drafts the full checklist and the 90-day notice letter: confirm account ownership, export all contacts, verify DSISD hosting/domain is NOT on GHL (only the form embed appears to be), inventory anything else live, plan the replacement form. Approval is J29.
J29:
Why: The notice clock shouldn't start until the DSF form swap (J12) is done — that sequencing keeps the DSISD funnel alive with zero dark days while the subscription winds down.

How: Waits on A24's plan AND J12. Confirm the export is complete and the new form is live, then send the drafted 90-day notice. Claude preps it; you hit send.
